title: Centralized & Structured Logging
description:
## Logs Across Many Containers

You run dozens of microservice containers across many hosts. Why is **centralized, structured logging** (using a system like ELK, Loki, or Splunk with JSON-formatted logs) considered best practice?

options.text: Because aggregating logs from all hosts/containers into one searchable system — with structured (JSON) fields — lets you search, filter and correlate events across services from a single place
Because keeping plain-text logs only on each container's local disk makes them faster to write and that's all that matters
Because structured logs let the application run without ever emitting any errors
Because a log aggregator replaces the need for metrics and tracing entirely

explanation: With many ephemeral containers spread across hosts, logs scattered on local disks are nearly impossible to investigate — and they vanish when a container dies. **Centralizing** them into one searchable store (ELK, Loki, Splunk) means you can query everything from one place. Making logs **structured** (JSON with consistent fields like `level`, `service`, `trace_id`) lets you filter and **correlate** events across services precisely, instead of grepping free-form text. Local-only plain text (b) defeats both goals; structured logging doesn't prevent errors (c); and logs complement — not replace — metrics and traces (d).

hints.title: Search and correlate
hints.body: Two wins: aggregation puts all logs in one queryable place, and structure (consistent JSON fields) makes filtering and cross-service correlation reliable.
